title: Characterization of Defect Levels in Doped and Undoped CuGaSe2 by Means of Photoluminescence Measurements
abstract:
phys. stat. sol. (a) 161, 301 (1997)
Subject classification: 71.55.Ht; 78.55Hx; S8.12; S8.16
Characterization of Defect Levels in Doped
and Undoped CuGaSe2 by Means
of Photoluminescence Measurements
J. H. SchoÈn1, O. Schenker, H. Riazi-Nejad, K. Friemelt, Ch. Kloc,
and E. Bucher
University of Konstanz, Faculty of Physics, P.O. Box 5560, D-78434 Konstanz, Germany
(Received November 15, 1996; in revised form January 29, 1997)
Photoluminescence properties of various as-grown, annealed, and doped CuGaSe2 samples have
been investigated in the range from liquid helium temperature to room temperature in order to
determine extrinsic and intrinsic defect levels of this material. The observed spectra of undoped
samples can be described using two acceptor and three donor levels. We propose VCu (55 meV) and
Sei (95 meV) as acceptor and VSe (80 and 110 meV) and Gai (110 to 120 meV) as donor levels,
respectively. The spectra of intentionally doped CuGaSe2 single crystals were dominated by broad
donor±acceptor pair transitions. The formation of extrinsic donor levels is in accordance with the
results of electrical measurements, which indicated a strong compensation of the p-type materials.
Sn-, Ge-, Si-, and B-doped crystals exhibited one broad emission band below 1.45 eV, whereas sam-
ples doped by group II atoms showed two radiative transitions. The activation energies for CdCu,
MgCu, ZnCu, and IIGa were estimated to 80, 90, 110, and 210 meV, respectively.
1. Introduction
Among the available alternative materials to Si, the ternary chalcopyrites of the Cu±
III±VI2-type appear to be very promising candidates for use in photovoltaic energy con-
version [1]. CuIn1 ÿ xGaxSe2 thin film solar cells reached efficiencies up to 17.7% [2], and
recently CuGaSe2/CdS/ZnO single crystal cells of 9.7% efficiency have been obtained
[3]. With its band gap of 1.73 eV CuGaSe2 could be a proper choice in photovoltaic
tandem systems combined with Si or CuInSe2 for example [4]. Although studies on
growth and properties of this compound have been reported, detailed investigations of
the fundamental defect and impurity levels are still required for further improvement of
solar energy conversion. Photoluminescence (PL) has been identified as a non-destruc-
tive, sensitive technique for the identification of these levels. However, previous PL stu-
dies on CuGaSe2 single crystals and thin films have shown a large variety of emissions
differing from sample to sample [5 to 15]. From this point of view further studies on
energy states and chemical nature of defects and impurities present in this promising
material are necessary. Furthermore, it was found that interdiffusion at the interface of
CuGaSe2 and the buffer or window layer, respectively, has a dominant impact on the
overall solar cell efficiency. Consequently, it seems to be essential to examine properties
of doped CuGaSe2 with respect to the use of new buffer layers and window materials, in
1 Phone: 07531/88 2082; Fax: 07531/88 3895
e-mail: Hendrik.Schoen@uni-konstanz.de
J. H. SchoÈn et al.: Characterization of Defect Levels in CuGaSe2
301
 particular since literature data about doping of CuGaSe2 single crystals are scarce
[16, 17].
In this study we report on excitation intensity and temperature dependent, steady-
state PL measurements on as grown CuGaSe2 single crystals grown by chemical vapor
transport (CVT) using iodine as transport agent and thin films prepared by rapid ther-
mal processing (RTP) of stacked elemental layers [18]. In addition, the influences of
heat treatments in different atmospheres (Cu, Ga, Se, vacuum) and doping by Si, Sn,
Ge, Zn, Mg, Cd, Al, and B on the PL properties of single crystals have been investi-
gated. On the one hand, the dopants were selected with respect to new, alternative
window and buffer materials, and on the other hand, in order to form donor levels in
CuGaSe2 to overcome the lack of n-type conductivity in this material. The results are
discussed taking into account the stoichiometry of the samples, formation energies of
the defect levels, and electrical measurements (specific resistivity, Hall effect) presented
earlier [19].
2. Experimental
2.1 Crystal growth
CuGaSe2 single crystals were grown from a stoichiometric mixture of the elements (6N
purity) by chemical vapor transport (CVT) at temperatures between 700 and 830 C
using iodine as transport agent. The p-type crystals had typical dimensions of
1  4  10 mm3, but crystals as large as 5  15  35 mm3 could be prepared after 2
months growth. In addition to conventional CVT growth, a modified semi-open growth
method of CuGaSe2 was performed. The stoichiometric mixture was put into the inner
of two evacuated quartz ampoules. The inner tube exhibits a very small hole, which
connects gases from the inside with the evacuated outer ampoule. Additionally, a strong
temperature gradient (500 to 750 C) was applied. This semi-open growth technique
offers impurities and non-stoichiometric components outgassing from the source the pos-
sibility to escape from the inner ampoule. Furthermore the mass transport is not limited
by these components. Therefore this method can avoid limitations of closed container
techniques as CVT.
Doping of the single crystals was performed either during CVT growth or by a subse-
quent diffusion process. Sn, Ge, Si, Al, B, Cd, Mg, and Zn were used as dopants. Pre-
vious to doping by diffusion the CuGaSe2 single crystals were cut into 0.5 mm thick
slices. After that a thin layer of the dopant was evaporated on top of them, followed by
a heat treatment at temperatures ranging from 200 to 400 C. This annealing step was
performed in an Ar:H atmosphere in a quartz tube placed in a horizontal furnace. Final-
ly the specimens were mechanically polished and etched in diluted NH3 and a solution of
bromine and methanol.
2.2 Thin film preparation
CuGaSe2 thin films were fabricated starting with a stack of elemental layers and then
using the RTP method. The elemental layers were deposited by thermal evaporation in
an Edwards E306A unit at a background pressure of 10ÿ4 Pa. The Cu and the Ga
layers were separated by an intermediate Se layer to prevent the formation of the
CuGa2 compound during the evaporation [18]. Mo-coated floating glass was used as a

In this study we report on excitation intensity and temperature dependent, steady-
state PL measurements on as grown CuGaSe2 single crystals grown by chemical vapor
transport (CVT) using iodine as transport agent and thin films prepared by rapid ther-
mal processing (RTP) of stacked elemental layers [18]. In addition, the influences of
heat treatments in different atmospheres (Cu, Ga, Se, vacuum) and doping by Si, Sn,
Ge, Zn, Mg, Cd, Al, and B on the PL properties of single crystals have been investi-
gated. On the one hand, the dopants were selected with respect to new, alternative
window and buffer materials, and on the other hand, in order to form donor levels in
CuGaSe2 to overcome the lack of n-type conductivity in this material. The results are
discussed taking into account the stoichiometry of the samples, formation energies of
the defect levels, and electrical measurements (specific resistivity, Hall effect) presented
CuGaSe2 single crystals were grown from a stoichiometric mixture of the elements (6N
purity) by chemical vapor transport (CVT) at temperatures between 700 and 830 C
using iodine as transport agent. The p-type crystals had typical dimensions of
1  4  10 mm3, but crystals as large as 5  15  35 mm3 could be prepared after 2
months growth. In addition to conventional CVT growth, a modified semi-open growth
method of CuGaSe2 was performed. The stoichiometric mixture was put into the inner
of two evacuated quartz ampoules. The inner tube exhibits a very small hole, which
connects gases from the inside with the evacuated outer ampoule. Additionally, a strong
temperature gradient (500 to 750 C) was applied. This semi-open growth technique
offers impurities and non-stoichiometric components outgassing from the source the pos-
sibility to escape from the inner ampoule. Furthermore the mass transport is not limited
by these components. Therefore this method can avoid limitations of closed container
Doping of the single crystals was performed either during CVT growth or by a subse-
quent diffusion process. Sn, Ge, Si, Al, B, Cd, Mg, and Zn were used as dopants. Pre-
vious to doping by diffusion the CuGaSe2 single crystals were cut into 0.5 mm thick
slices. After that a thin layer of the dopant was evaporated on top of them, followed by
a heat treatment at temperatures ranging from 200 to 400 C. This annealing step was
performed in an Ar:H atmosphere in a quartz tube placed in a horizontal furnace. Final-
ly the specimens were mechanically polished and etched in diluted NH3 and a solution of
CuGaSe2 thin films were fabricated starting with a stack of elemental layers and then
using the RTP method. The elemental layers were deposited by thermal evaporation in
an Edwards E306A unit at a background pressure of 10ÿ4 Pa. The Cu and the Ga
layers were separated by an intermediate Se layer to prevent the formation of the
CuGa2 compound during the evaporation [18]. Mo-coated floating glass was used as a
J. H. SchoÈn, O. Schenker, H. Riazi-Nejad, K. Friemelt, Ch. Kloc, and E. Bucher
substrate material. The precursors were annealed in a graphite box at 550 C for 5 min
in Ar atmosphere using a commercial RTP furnace SHS-100 supplied by AST. Second-
ary phases as Cu2 ÿ xSe were removed by chemical etching (10% KCN for 3 min). X-ray
diffraction patterns of etched thin films indicated polycrystalline single phase thin films.
2.3 Photoluminescence measurements
A 40 mW Krypton-Ion-Laser (Spectra Physics model 165 ion laser) at a wavelength of
568.2 nm was used as excitation source for steady-state PL measurements. The laser
beam was focussed onto the sample with a diameter of about 100 mm and the lumines-
cent light was detected by a GCA/McPherson Instrument 1 m scanning monochromator
(Czerny-Turner type) and a photomultiplier of S1 characteristics.
PL measurements have been done on as grown CuGaSe2 single crystals grown by CVT
under different conditions (see Table 1) and stoichiometric thin films in the temperature
range between 2 K and room temperature. General features were excitonic emissions or
band-to-band recombination (1.72 eV) and transitions involving shallow defect levels
(1.60 to 1.71 eV). X-ray fluorescence experiments revealed no significant amounts of ex-
trinsic dopants like transition metals. Therefore the PL emissions of as grown CuGaSe2
samples are attributed intrinsic native defects. Fig. 1 shows PL spectra at 6 K of differ-
ent undoped CuGaSe2 samples. The near band edge emission of CuGaSe2 at 2 K could
be resolved to consist of at least two transitions (see Fig. 2). One emission line at 1.721
eV is attributed to the radiative decay of the free exciton ground state. This was con-
firmed by excitation intensity dependent measurements. The intensity of this transition
decreased with increasing temperature. Binding energies of 5 to 16 meV have been re-
ported for the free exciton in CuGaSe2 [20, 21]. Taking into account a binding energy of
the free exciton of approximately 10 meV, the band gap is then approximated to
1.731 eV. The origin of the second radiative transition at low temperatures at 1.726 eV
Characterization of Defect Levels in Doped and Undoped CuGaSe2
Growth parameters of various CuGaSe2 single crystals (SC) and thin films (TF)
J. H. SchoÈn, O. Schenker, H. Riazi-Nejad, K. Friemelt, Ch. Kloc, and E. Bucher

Fig. 2. Near band edge emissions of Cu-
GaSe2 single crystals and a thin film
is not clearly identified until now. In the case of CuGaSe2 thin films grown on Mo-
coated glass the emission lines were shifted to lower energies (1.725 and 1.720 eV, re-
spectively), which could be caused by lattice strain in the thin films [14]. This is
strengthened by X-ray diffraction, which indicated slightly different lattice constants for
single crystals and thin films grown on Mo-coated glass substrates. At higher tempera-
tures band-to-band recombination dominant, which could be determined by an exact
analysis of the line shape [22]. A weak peak at 1.711 eV was observed in only few sam-
ples and was identified as free-to-bound transition by means of excitation intensity de-
pendent measurements. Since Hall effect data revealed the presence of an acceptor level
with an activation energy less than 25 meV [19, 23], we tentatively ascribe this emission
line to an acceptor-to-conduction band transition. Ga vacancies could act as shallow
acceptor levels in this material. On the other hand, a donor activation energy of 20 meV
has been reported [24] and the formation of VCu±GaCu defect pairs in Cu-poor materials
(for example CVT-grown crystals) is supposed to be highly probable [25]. These are
some hints to ascribe this level to a shallow donor as the GaCu antisite defect. Further
investigations have to be carried out to determine the origin of this emission. The strong
peak at energies varying from 1.67 to 1.68 eV was observed in all single crystalline sam-
ples. Some samples showed a blue shift of this emission with increasing excitation inten-
sity (see Fig. 3), which would indicate a simple donor±acceptor pair transition. The
Characterization of Defect Levels in Doped and Undoped CuGaSe2

intensity of the 1.675 eV emission
at 6 K for various single crystalline
energy of such transitions is given by the following expression:
where EG is the band gap, EA and ED are the acceptor and donor binding energies,
respectively, er is the dielectric constant, and r is the pair separation. The last term fr
is a quantum mechanical correction, which can be neglected if r is large compared with
the radii of the donor and acceptor states. Due to thermal emission of carriers bound to
acceptor and donor levels, respectively, we expect an increase in the pair separation r
with increasing temperature. As a result of this the peak should shift to lower energies
in addition to the shrinking of the band gap. On the other hand, a shift to higher ener-
gies with increasing temperature was detected. This could be explained assuming a
superposition of a donor±acceptor pair and a free-to-bound transition. The energy of
free-to-bound transitions is given by the following expression:
The intensity of the donor±acceptor pair transition decreases with increasing tempera-
ture due to thermal emission of bound carriers. Furthermore, the intensity of the free-to-
bound transition is increased. In the case of a small separation between the two transi-
tions the superposition of both peaks can only be detected as a broad emission line. Since
the energy of the free-to-bound transition is higher (see equations (1) and (2)), the line
shifts to higher energies as the temperature is rising. Hence, we ascribe this emission line
at 1.675 eV to an acceptor-to-conduction band transition which is superimposed with a
donor±acceptor pair transition in some samples similar to the results of Yamada et al.
[26]. Taking into account the band gap of 1.731 eV the activation energy of the acceptor
level can be estimated to 55 meV. This value is in good agreement with other PL studies
[9, 10] and Hall effect measurements [19, 23]. The defect level is ascribed to the Cu va-
cancy which is the most probable defect in this material. The donor level, which is in-
volved in the pair transition could be identical to the defect of the shallow free-to-bound
transition at 1.711 eV, but further experiments have to be done to identify this level. The
peak at 1.64 eV of sample ER26 (see Fig. 1) showed the same temperature and excitation
intensity dependence as the emission line at 1.675 eV and is therefore ascribed to be a
longitudinal optical (LO) phonon replica of this peak. The value of 35 meV for the LO
phonon is in accordance with literature [27 to 29]. In the case of the other samples the
emission below 1.67 eV can be explained using the model of Masse [9] containing an
acceptor level of 55 meV (VCu), and two donor levels of 80 and 110 meV, respectively
(VSe). The deep level could be related to vacancy complexes or other more complicated
defects than point defects. Therefore, four transitions contribute to the PL emission be-
low 1.67 eV: two donor-to-valence band transitions (1.65 and 1.62 eV) and two donor±
acceptor pair transitions (approximately 1.625 and 1.59 eV taking into account the Cou-
lomb interaction [19, 30, 31]). These VSe-related emissions were more pronounced in
melt-grown crystals than in CVT-grown ones [5, 32]. This can be explained by the lower
Se2 partial pressure due to the lower growth temperature during the process. Therefore,
the formation of Se vacancies seems to be more probable at lower growth temperatures.
As a result of this the density of electrical compensating donor levels is higher for non-
CVT grown crystals resulting in low specific conductivities [17, 33]. The enhanced forma-
tion of Se vacancies could also explain that the PL spectra of thin films grown at 550 C
J. H. SchoÈn, O. Schenker, H. Riazi-Nejad, K. Friemelt, Ch. Kloc, and E. Bucher
were dominated by VSe-related emissions rather than by VCu-related emissions, although
all CuGaSe2 samples show p-type conductivity, which is generally attributed to the for-
mation of Cu vacancies. Furthermore, the intensity of the VSe emissions is influenced by
the applied temperature gradient. Whereas crystals grown with a very small gradient
(ER24, ER26) exhibited almost no emissions, samples grown with a large gradient
(ER39, ER40) exhibited very well pronounced emission bands below 1.65 eV, which is
shown in Fig. 1. On the other hand, the PL spectrum of sample EFU4 is dominated by
VCu-related emissions, which is in contrast to the large temperature gradient of 250 K
applied during the growth. This could be caused by the different atmosphere inside the
inner ampoule compared to standard CVT growth due to the modified growth technique.
The results of PL measurements on as-grown CuGaSe2 samples are summarized in the
first column of a proposed energy band diagram for CuGaSe2 (see Fig. 7).
Some crystals were annealed in tubes sealed in vacuum, with Cu, Ga, Se, or alone at
temperatures between 300 and 650 C for times of 2 to 60 h. Vacuum annealing in-
creases the concentration of VSe-related emissions below 1.65 eV. A similar behaviour
was seen for samples annealed in excess of Cu, which is shown in Fig. 4. The concentra-
tion of VCu decreased and the amount of VSe increased, resulting in the disappearance of
the 1.675 eV emission, whereas the emission at 1.645 eV became dominant. In compar-
Characterization of Defect Levels in Doped and Undoped CuGaSe2
Fig. 4. Normalized PL spectra of as-
ison to samples annealed in vacuum (without Cu) the free-to-bound transitions were
more intense than the donor±acceptor pair transitions due to the incorporation of Cu
atoms. This behaviour is in accordance with the electrical properties. Both types of
annealing (minimum of Se pressure) result in an electrical compensation and a reduction
of the carrier concentration. But in the case of Cu-annealed samples this compensation
was much more pronounced. Annealing of CuGaSe2 crystals in Se atmosphere reduced
the PL intensity by a factor of 10 with an asymmetric emission band at 1.663 eV, which
is in accordance with literature data [10, 30]. Although this emission showed a blue shift
with increasing excitation intensity, we have reason to assume that this band is formed
by two independent free-to-bound transitions [31]. This was confirmed by fitting two
Gaussian-shaped peaks as approximation for these two emission bands. The shift of this
emission could originate from a change of the relative intensities of the peaks 1.672 and
1.633 eV. Hall measurements on samples annealed in excess Se showed an increase in
the carrier concentration [34]. Samples with very high concentrations also showed a
change in activation energy of 80 to 90 meV [35]. Hence, we suggest both defect levels
to be acceptors. The Cu vacancy and Se interstitials, although their formation energy
should be very high (22.4 eV in the case of CuInSe2) [36], with activation energies of
approximately 60 and 95 meV, respectively, are supposed to be the corresponding de-
fects. The formation of Sei and Si has also been proposed for CuInSe2 and CuInS2 single
crystals [37]. Annealing in Ga atmosphere caused a broad emission band at energies
varying from 1.58 to 1.63 eV depending on time and temperature of annealing. The
band shifted very strongly to lower energies as the excitation intensity was decreased
(up to 12 meV per decade of excitation intensity) and is therefore attributed to a do-
nor±acceptor pair transition. Considering the variation in Coulomb energy (see equation
(1)) the variation of the peak position for the different samples could be explained. The
emission energy of the pairs at infinite separation was estimated as 1.56 eV from low
excitation intensity measurements. Electrical measurements indicated the formation of
donor levels. Taking into account the activation energy of VCu (55 meV) we can calcu-
late a donor level at 110 to 120 meV for Ga-annealed materials. GaCu and Gai have
been reported as donor levels in this material with activation energies of 20 and
110 meV, respectively [24]. Hence, we tentatively ascribe this level to Ga interstitials due
to the high activation energy of this defect. There has been no sign for an increased
formation of the shallow level (1.711 eV emission) which could be due to GaCu.
The second column of the band energy diagram (Fig. 7) shows the defect levels identi-
fied by PL measurements of annealed samples.
CuGaSe2 samples doped by diffusion and during CVT growth showed different PL spec-
tra. Due to the thermal annealing diffusion doped specimen showed transitions due to the
formation of Se vacancies [19], which were similar to vacuum annealed samples, in addition
to dopant-related emissions. Therefore, we focused our studies on CVT-doped samples to
examine the influence of the dopants on the radiative recombination of the samples.
3.2.1 Doping by group IV elements
The PL of samples doped with group IV elements (Si, Ge, and Sn) was dominated by
broad (120 to 140 meV) donor±acceptor pair transitions at 1.3 eV (Sn, Ge) and 1.4 eV
J. H. SchoÈn, O. Schenker, H. Riazi-Nejad, K. Friemelt, Ch. Kloc, and E. Bucher
(Si), respectively. Due to the low sensitivity of the photomultiplier in this energy range
the peak positions could not be determined with high accuracy. Assuming VCu as accep-
tor defect in these transitions the activation energies of the deep levels could be approxi-
mated as 300 meV for Si-doped samples and 400 meV for Sn- and Ge-doped CuGaSe2.
These donor levels are ascribed to an extrinsic dopant, since the crystals showed strong
electrical compensation [19, 23]. The occupation of a cation site by the group IV element
could act as simple explanation for these donor levels. Similar broad emissions involving
deep levels have been reported for CuGaS2 single crystals grown from Sn flux [38].
3.2.2 Doping by group III elements
Doping with elements from group III (Al, B) showed two different spectra. The PL
spectra of B-doped CuGaSe2 was characterized by a broad donor±acceptor pair transi-
tion at 1.45 eV, whereas Al-doped samples exhibited emissions similar to the line at
1.675 eV of undoped single crystals. The energetic position of this line shifted to slightly
higher energies than for undoped specimen depending on the Al content. Therefore, we
suggest the growth of the quaternary alloy CuGa1 ÿ xAlxSe2 in the presence of Al, which
causes a shift in the band gap from 1.73 eV (CuGaSe2) to 2.72 eV (CuAlSe2) [20, 39,
40]. The temperature dependence of the PL spectra is shown in Fig. 5. An activation
energy of 55 meV for VCu was obtained from thermal quenching of this emission, which
is in accordance with the value evaluated for undoped samples. The strong contrast of
Characterization of Defect Levels in Doped and Undoped CuGaSe2

to 150 K) PL spectra of Al-doped
CuGaSe2. The insert shows the ther-
mal quenching of the integrated PL
intensity as function of the recipro-
cal temperature. An activation en-
the PL spectra of B- and Al-doped samples could be related to the large difference of
the atomic radii of these atoms. Due to its small size boron could easily occupy intersti-
tial lattice sites, which may act as donor levels in CuGaSe2. Assuming a similar transi-
tion between these donor levels and the Cu vacancy as in the case of group IV doped
CuGaSe2 an activation energy of approximately 250 meV can be estimated.
The results of doping by group III and IV impurities are summarized in the third
column of the proposed energy band diagram in Fig. 7.
J. H. SchoÈn, O. Schenker, H. Riazi-Nejad, K. Friemelt, Ch. Kloc, and E. Bucher
Fig. 6. Normalized PL intensity as function of temperature and energy for a) an Mg-doped and b)
an as-grown CuGaSe2 (ER40) sample
3.2.3 Doping by group II elements
PL spectra of CuGaSe2 samples doped by group II atoms (Mg, Cd, Zn) were dominated
by the presence of two broad donor±acceptor pair transitions compared to the emission
at 1.675 eV and the band-to-band transition for undoped samples (see Fig. 6a and b).
The higher energetic donor±acceptor pair transition (1.57 eV for Zn, 1.60 eV for Mg,
and 1.61 eV for Cd-doped samples) was dominant at low temperatures (2 to 100 K),
whereas the second emission band (1.40 eV for Zn, 1.42 eV for Mg, and 1.44 eV for Cd-
doped samples) was stronger at temperatures above approximately 100 K. This behavior
shows some similarities to that of Zn-doped CuGaS2 single crystals [41, 42]. Since group
II atoms have shown an amphoteric behavior in Cu±III±VI2 compounds [43, 44], we
assume the formation of one donor and one acceptor level due to the incorporation of
group II atoms. IICu (donor) and IIGa (acceptor) seem to be the most possible candidates
for these levels, since the dopant atoms could easily occupy the present Cu- and Ga-
vacancy states, since annealing experiments of CuAlS2 in Cd, Zn, Cd/Al, and Zn/Al
atmospheres showed the formation of IIAl and IICu defects [43]. Thermal quenching of
the high energetic peak showed activation energies between 45 and 60 meV. Therefore
we ascribe the shallow acceptor level to the intrinsic defect of the Cu vacancy. Taking
into account the activation energy of about 55 meV and a Coulomb energy of 10 to
20 meV we can calculate the corresponding donor levels to approximately 80 meV
(CdCu), 90 meV (MgCu), and 110 meV (ZnCu) using equation (1). The second emission
line can be interpreted as IICu±IIGa donor±acceptor pair transition. The activation ener-
gies of CdGa, MgGa, and ZnGa can be estimated to about 210 meV using the calculated
activation energies of IICu. In comparison activation energies of 100 meV (ZnCu) and
230 meV (ZnAl) have been obtained from photoluminescence measurements for Zn-doped
Characterization of Defect Levels in Doped and Undoped CuGaSe2
Fig. 7. Proposed energy band diagram of as-grown, annealed, and doped CuGaSe2 single crystals
The resulting energy levels of CuGaSe2 doped by group IV elements are shown in the
last column of the band diagram in Fig. 7.
Photoluminescence properties of various as grown, annealed, and doped CuGaSe2 sam-
ples have been investigated in order to determine defect levels present in this material.
PL spectra of as grown samples are dominated by free-to-bound and donor±acceptor
pair transitions involving shallow levels of VCu (55 meV) and VSe (80 and 110 meV).
The influence of VSe on PL properties becomes stronger for crystals grown at lower
temperatures and thin films prepared at 550 C due to the reduced Se2 pressure during
the crystal growth. In few samples a free-to-bound transition at 1.711 eV is observable,
which we tentatively ascribe to the acceptor level of VGa. The activation energies of Sei
(acceptor) and Gai (donor) were estimated to 95 meV and 110 to 120 meV, respectively,
taking into account results of annealing in Ga and Se excess and formation energies of
The PL spectra of single crystals doped during CVT-growth by Sn, Ge, Si, B, Zn, Cd,
and Mg were dominated by donor±acceptor pair transitions due to the formation of ex-
trinsic donor levels. This confirms the results of electrical measurements, which showed a
strong compensation for these p-type samples. Sn-, Ge-, Si-, and B-doped crystals showed
one broad emission band below 1.45 eV, whereas samples doped by group II atoms exhib-
ited two radiative transitions. The activation energies for CdCu, MgCu, ZnCu, and IIGa
were estimated to 80, 90, 110, and 210 meV, respectively, taking into account a donor
and an acceptor level related to the dopant and the intrinsic acceptor level of VCu.
The assignment of the intrinsic and extrinsic defects is partly speculative. We took
into account the formation energies of the defects, stoichiometry and resistivity of the
samples, and the atomic radii of the dopants in order to ascribe the evaluated activation
energies to the most reasonable defects. All results of our model are summarized in a
proposed energy band diagram for CuGaSe2 single crystals in Fig. 7.
In order to identify the shallow level in the spectra of some undoped samples or to
examine the detailed structure of the emission between 1.58 and 1.65 eV further studies
are necessary. Time dependent PL studies could also provide further information about
doped crystals. In addition, more detailed studies on thin films, where the Cu/Ga atom-
ic ratio could be controlled precisely in a wide range from Cu-rich to Ga-rich as in the
case of CuInSe2 [47, 48], could give further data of intrinsic defect levels.
